DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-09-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Ian Diaz, Esq. (858.868.5000; id@pinlegal.com) of Pinnacle Legal P.C. for Plaintiff.
R. Max Segal, Esq. (909.367.8425; max@lawpla.com) of Law Office of Parag L. Amin, P.C. for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KEVIN VED'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant Ved's demurrer to the first and second causes of action of the first amended complaint for intentional misrepresentation is overruled. The Court notes that the notice of demurrer refers only to the second and third causes of action.

The sham pleading doctrine does not apply. "Under the sham pleading doctrine, plaintiffs are precluded from amending complaints to omit harmful allegations, without explanation, from previous complaints to avoid attacks raised in demurrers or motions for summary judgment." (Deveny v. Entropin, Inc. (2006) 139 Cal.App.4th 408, 425.) Here, plaintiff alleges that defendant agreed that the transaction was a loan but harbored a secret intent to treat the loan as an investment so he would not pay it back. (FAC 19, 24-25 [at pg. 5], 31.) There is no inconsistency in the allegations nor is plaintiff attempting to avoid a prior harmful allegation.

Plaintiff's second cause of action sufficiently supports a claim for fraud (False Promise--CACI 1902). Plaintiff avers that defendant promised that he would repay the $275,000 loan by June 30, 2019, but defendant had no intent to perform that promise, which is evidenced by defendant's current characterization of the transaction as an investment. (FAC, 24.) Plaintiff sufficiently specifies the false promise. (See FAC, 19; Ex. 1, Document 1 [Promissory Note].) Moreover, less specificity is required where "the facts lie more in the knowledge of the opposite party." (Tenet HealthSystem Desert, Inc. v. Blue Cross of Cal. (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 821, 838.) Plaintiff has "provide[d] enough information for respondents to know what purported falsehoods they must defend against." (Murphy v. BDO Seidman, LLP (2003) 113 Cal.App.4th 687, 693.)

The demurrer to the third cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is sustained without leave to amend. Pursuant to CACI 1903, plaintiff must allege that defendant represented to plaintiff that a fact was true and that representation was untrue. Here, plaintiff contends that defendant's representation (the parties entered into a loan transaction) is actually true. The cause of action is therefore inapposite. (See Tarmann v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. (1991) 2 Cal.App.4th 153, 158-159 [explaining that a negligent misrepresentation must ordinarily be as to past or existing material facts and dismissing negligent misrepresentation claim because there is no such claim as a "negligent false promise"].)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling via email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice And Motion To Set Aside Default; Points And Athorities; Declaration
Off Calendar Jan-13-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance is made in this matter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant Kevin Ved's motion to set aside the default entered on August 13, 2020 is granted. The February 9, 2021 hearing on plaintiff's motion for default judgment in Dept. 514 is vacated.

"When service of a summons has not resulted in actual notice to a party in time to defend the action and a default or default judgment has been entered against him or her in the action, he or she may serve and file a notice of motion to set aside the default or default judgment and for leave to defend the action." (Code Civ. Proc. section 473.5.) Defendant testifies that he "never received the Summons or Complaint" that was mailed to his former residence at 2443 38th Ave, and that he did not live or work at that address. (Def.'s Decl., 14.) Plaintiff does not offer any evidence that counters Defendant's declaration that he did not have actual notice and was not living at the 38th Ave address at the time of the purported substitute service. Moreover, the declaration of due diligence reflects that Plaintiff's process server was told by the purported "co-occupant" that Defendant didn't live at that address.

Plaintiff's argument that "Defendant does not refute that he was in contact with Plaintiff and aware of the matter in dispute" is inconsequential. (See Plf.'s Opp., 2:26-27.) "We hold that if the court has acquired jurisdiction, i.e., summons has been served, but service of summons has not resulted in actual notice to a defendant, although the defendant has acquired actual knowledge of the action from another source, this does not preclude a defendant from seeking relief under section 473.5." (Olvera v. Olvera (1991) 232 Cal.App.3d 32, 40.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the adopted ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
